issues.title: ⚡ Annual Town Meeting Begins May 26 — 25 Warrant Articles Including Zoning Changes
issues.summary:
⚡ URGENT (May 19, 2026): Brookline's 2026 Annual Town Meeting begins Tuesday May 26 at 7:00pm in the Brookline High School Auditorium — 6 DAYS AWAY. The warrant contains 25 articles including the Town budget, 10 articles proposing changes to general or zoning bylaws, and 6 resolution articles. The Select Board has added a Special Town Meeting within the Annual, adding 2 articles to consider Chestnut Hill West rezoning.

KEY DEADLINES:
• PASSED (May 18): Deadline to file proposed amendments to Articles 3 and 8 (noon) — this deadline has passed
• ⚡ TODAY (May 20) by noon: Combined Report Supplement deadline
• ⚡ TODAY (May 20) 6-8pm: DPW Public Forum on Zoom
• May 22 by 9am: Motions for articles heard on the first night (May 26) must be filed
• ⚡ May 26: Town Meeting begins — 6 DAYS AWAY
• Reserved evenings: May 26, 27, 28, June 2, 3, 4

The meeting will be held in hybrid format with a remote attendance option, though organizers strongly encourage in-person attendance. The Combined Report has been posted with recommendations from the Select Board and Advisory Committee on each article.

This is Brookline's primary democratic institution — all major policy decisions flow through Town Meeting, including zoning changes that determine housing production capacity.

**May 23 update:** ⚡ TOWN MEETING IS 3 DAYS AWAY (May 26). Motion filing deadline for first-night articles was May 22 at 9am — now passed. The Special Town Meeting within the Annual will consider Chestnut Hill West rezoning: the proposed Chestnut Hill Commercial (CHC) Overlay District covers 27.8 acres over six blocks along Route 9, allowing buildings up to 150-175 feet (12-14 stories) on some parcels. A two-thirds majority is required for approval, and vocal opposition over height and traffic impacts is expected. This is the most consequential zoning vote of the year.

**May 24 update:** ⚡ TOWN MEETING IS 2 DAYS AWAY (May 26, 7pm, Brookline High School Auditorium). The Special Town Meeting within the Annual will consider the Chestnut Hill Commercial (CHC) Overlay District — 27.8 acres over six blocks along Route 9, with four subdistricts allowing buildings up to 150-175 feet (12-14 stories) on large catalytic parcels. Two-thirds majority required. Vocal opposition expected over height and traffic. This is the most consequential zoning vote of the year — and potentially the most important MBTA Communities Act compliance vote in Brookline's history.

**May 25 update:** ⚡ TOWN MEETING STARTS TOMORROW (May 26, 7pm, Brookline High School Auditorium). The warrant contains 25 articles including the Town budget, 10 zoning/bylaw changes, and 6 resolutions. ⚡ The CHC Overlay District vote is expected Thursday May 28 — City Realty's proposal for 1280-1330 Boylston Street includes three buildings (14, 12, and 7 stories) with a 200-room hotel, 266 apartments and condos, medical office space, and ground-floor retail. Brookline.News reports 42 Town Meeting members from South Brookline have registered formal opposition. Two-thirds majority is required for zoning changes — the opposition count already threatens the supermajority threshold. This is Brookline's biggest potential redevelopment project in decades and a test case for MBTA Communities Act compliance. Hybrid format available but in-person attendance strongly encouraged.

**May 26 update:** ⚡ TOWN MEETING STARTS TONIGHT (7pm, Brookline High School Auditorium). The warrant contains 25 articles including the Town budget, 10 zoning/bylaw changes, and 6 resolutions. The Special Town Meeting within the Annual adds 2 articles for Chestnut Hill West rezoning. ⚡ The CHC Overlay District vote is expected Thursday May 28 (2 DAYS) — City Realty's 1280-1330 Boylston Street proposal (14, 12, 7 stories; 266 housing units, 200-room hotel). 42 South Brookline TM members have registered formal opposition. Two-thirds majority required. A rival developer's last-minute objection could further delay the vote. This is Brookline's biggest potential redevelopment in decades. Attend in person if possible — close votes require physical presence.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ TOWN MEETING CONTINUES TONIGHT (May 27, Brookline High School Auditorium). Annual Town Meeting began last night (May 26) with 25 warrant articles on the docket. The meeting continues tonight and is reserved through potentially June 4. The CHC Overlay District vote is expected Thursday May 28 — TOMORROW. The May 5 tax override passed overwhelmingly: $23.25M over three years, approved 8,675 to 5,732 with the highest-ever local turnout (34%+). The override funds schools ($17.94M) and town departments ($5.31M), avoiding hundreds of teacher layoffs and Fire Department cuts. Property taxes will increase approximately 18% over 3 years. Hybrid format available but in-person attendance strongly encouraged for close zoning votes.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: CHC OVERLAY DISTRICT VOTE TONIGHT. Town Meeting will vote today (Thursday May 28) on whether to rezone 27.8 acres on Route 9 in Chestnut Hill. City Realty's proposal: three buildings (14, 12, and 7 stories) at 1280-1330 Boylston Street containing a 200-room hotel, 266 apartments/condos, medical office space, and ground-floor retail. Two-thirds supermajority required. ⚡ PROPONENTS APPEAR TO HAVE THE VOTES after a recent agreement brought on board neighbors who had withheld support over height concerns. Fiscal impact: $4.2-6.3M/year in net new tax revenue to the town. This is Brookline's biggest potential redevelopment in decades and a test case for MBTA Communities Act compliance.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ MASSIVE VICTORY: Town Meeting voted 217-20 on May 29 to approve the CHC Overlay District rezoning — far exceeding the required two-thirds supermajority. City Realty's proposal for 1280-1330 Boylston Street (three buildings of 14, 12, and 7 stories containing a 200-room hotel, 266 apartments/condos, medical office space, and ground-floor retail) can now proceed. City Realty CDO Clifford Kensington said he was 'excited to continue moving forward' and didn't expect the margin to be that large. Fiscal impact: $4.2-6.3M/year in net new tax revenue. This is Brookline's biggest redevelopment approval in decades and a watershed moment for MBTA Communities Act compliance. Town Meeting continues through June 4 for remaining warrant articles including ADU updates (WA14) and zoning bylaw amendments (WA15), both with strong support.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ MASSIVE VICTORY: Town Meeting voted 217-20 on May 29 to approve the CHC Overlay District rezoning — far exceeding the required two-thirds supermajority. City Realty's proposal for 1280-1330 Boylston Street (three buildings of 14, 12, and 7 stories containing a 200-room hotel, 266 apartments/condos, medical office space, and ground-floor retail) can now proceed. City Realty CDO Clifford Kensington said he was 'excited to continue moving forward' and didn't expect the margin to be that large. Fiscal impact: $4.2-6.3M/year in net new tax revenue. This is Brookline's biggest redevelopment approval in decades and a watershed moment for MBTA Communities Act compliance. Town Meeting continues through June 4 for remaining warrant articles including ADU updates (WA14) and zoning bylaw amendments (WA15), both with strong support.

**June 4 update:** MAJOR — Town Meeting approved Route 9 rezoning 217-20 (well above two-thirds threshold) for City Realty Group's mixed-use development: 3 buildings, 200-room hotel, 266 apartments. Largest development in Brookline's history. 26 Pleasant Street (103 apartments, 15 affordable) POSTPONED to fall Town Meeting. $23.5M operating override on ballot. Town Meeting continues through June 2-4 if needed.

**June 7 update:** Town Meeting wrapped up its spring session through June 4-5. Key outcomes beyond the CHC Overlay District (217-20): WA14 (Accessory Dwelling Unit updates) and WA15 (inclusionary zoning cash payments for sub-20-unit developments) both endorsed by Planning Board and expected to pass with strong support. WA16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) was NOT MOVED and postponed to fall Town Meeting for further developer and community engagement. The fall session will be the next major opportunity for additional housing production.

**June 10 update:** Town Meeting underway — Chestnut Hill West rezoning APPROVED on May 28, rezoning the commercial area along Boylston Street/Route 9 near the Newton border for multi-use development with housing. Voters overwhelmingly approved a tax override to stave off cuts to schools and town departments. Continuation sessions were reserved for June 2-4 if needed. The warrant contained 25 articles including the Town budget, 10 bylaw/zoning amendments, and 6 resolutions.

**June 11 update:** Town Meeting concluded — sessions ran May 26-28 and reserved June 2-4 if needed. Key outcomes: Chestnut Hill West rezoning APPROVED on May 28 (rezoning commercial area along Boylston Street/Route 9 near Newton border for multi-use development with housing). Tax override overwhelmingly approved. Article 14 updated Accessory Dwelling Unit rules. Article 15 amended zoning to restore cash-in-lieu payments for inclusionary units in sub-20-unit developments. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street, 103 apartments including 15 affordable) POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting. The warrant contained 25 articles: Town budget, 10 bylaw/zoning amendments, and 6 resolutions.

**June 12 update:** Town Meeting concluded. Key outcomes: Chestnut Hill West rezoning APPROVED (May 28) — rezoning commercial area along Boylston Street/Route 9 for multi-use development with housing. Tax override overwhelmingly approved. Article 14 updated ADU rules. Article 15 restored cash-in-lieu payments for inclusionary units in sub-20-unit developments. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street, 103 apartments including 15 affordable) POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting — this would have been the most significant new housing project. No further actions until fall session.

**June 13 update:** Town Meeting concluded. No new developments. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) officially POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting (typically November). Developer and community expected to engage in interim discussions to refine the proposal before fall. Chestnut Hill West rezoning APPROVED. Tax override approved. Updated ADU rules (Article 14) and restored inclusionary payments (Article 15) in effect.

**June 14 update:** No new developments. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting (typically November). Developer and community expected to engage in interim discussions. CHC Overlay District (Chestnut Hill West rezoning) approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect.

**June 15 update:** No new developments. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting (typically November). CHC Overlay District approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect.

**June 16 update:** No new developments. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting (typically November). Developer and community expected to engage in interim discussions. CHC Overlay District approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect.

**June 17 update:** ⚡ Community Housing funding review TODAY (June 17) at 2 PM on Zoom — Community Preservation Act funding allocations under discussion. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting (typically November). CHC Overlay District approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect. Annual Town Meeting concluded June 4.

**June 18 update:** Community Housing funding review held yesterday (June 17) at 2 PM on Zoom — Community Preservation Act funding allocations discussed. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting (typically November). CHC Overlay District approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect. Annual Town Meeting concluded June 4.

**June 19 update:** Annual Town Meeting concluded June 4. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting. CHC Overlay District approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect. No further action until fall.

**June 22 update:** Annual Town Meeting concluded June 4. Article 16 (26 Pleasant Street — 103 apartments including 15 affordable) remains POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting. CHC Overlay District approved 217-20. Updated ADU rules and restored inclusionary payments in effect. No further action until fall.

**June 23 update:** CHC Overlay District APPROVED by Town Meeting 217-20 on May 28 — landslide victory for pro-housing forces. Implementation phase underway for 266-unit mixed-use district along Boylston Street (Route 9). No further Town Meeting action needed. 26 Pleasant Street (Article 16): POSTPONED to fall Special Town Meeting as expected. The 103-unit project (15 affordable) will return when proponents complete additional community outreach. Override APPROVED $23.25M over 3 years by vote of 8,675-5,732. Highest-ever local turnout (34%). Override funds now secured for FY2027-FY2029.

**June 28 update:** ⚡ Annual Town Meeting approved Chestnut Hill Commercial Overlay District in landslide 217-20 vote — authorizing Select Board to enter agreements for 1280-1330 Boylston St redevelopment. Overlay supports mixed-use transformation with new housing, commercial, and public space. Brookline and Newton establishing Route 9 transportation working group. Housing Advisory Board to develop affordability guidelines for overlay incentives.

public_comment.instructions: Amendment deadline for Articles 3 and 8 has PASSED. ⚡ TODAY (May 20): DPW Public Forum 6-8pm on Zoom; Combined Report Supplement deadline at noon. Motions for first-night articles: May 22 by 9am. Town Meeting begins May 26 — 6 DAYS AWAY. Town Meeting members can attend in person or remotely. Warrant and Combined Report available at brooklinema.gov.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ⚡ TOWN MEETING STARTS TONIGHT (May 26, 7pm). This is Brookline's most important democratic event of the year — 10 of the 25 articles propose changes to general or zoning bylaws, directly affecting housing production capacity. The CHC Overlay District vote (expected Thursday May 28) is the biggest zoning decision: City Realty's proposal for 266 apartments/condos plus a 200-room hotel on Route 9. But 42 TM members from South Brookline have registered formal opposition — threatening the required two-thirds supermajority. If you're a Town Meeting member, in-person attendance is critical for close votes. The zoning articles determine whether Brookline evolves toward more housing or remains locked in its historically NIMBY posture. ⚡ THE VOTE PASSED 217-20 — a landslide. The feared opposition bloc of 42 South Brookline members was overwhelmed. This is Brookline's biggest pro-housing vote in a generation and proves that even in historically NIMBY communities, organized pro-housing advocacy can win decisively. The 266 units plus hotel represent meaningful housing production and $4.2-6.3M/year in tax revenue. Now monitor implementation — permitting timelines, construction start, and whether opponents attempt legal challenges. ⚡ THE VOTE PASSED 217-20 — a landslide. This is Brookline's biggest pro-housing vote in a generation and proves that even in historically NIMBY communities, organized pro-housing advocacy can win decisively. issues.title: Possible Budget Override on May 2026 Ballot — Town Fiscal Pressure
issues.summary:
DEVELOPING (2026): Town officials have been planning for a possible Proposition 2½ override vote on the May 2026 ballot to address Brookline's growing fiscal pressures. Massachusetts law limits annual property tax increases to 2.5% unless voters approve an override. Brookline faces rising costs for schools, infrastructure, and public services that outpace the 2.5% cap. An override would allow the town to raise property taxes beyond the cap to fund services that would otherwise face cuts. Override votes are politically sensitive in Brookline — the town has a history of approving them but each one generates significant debate about spending priorities and tax burden.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ OVERRIDE PASSED OVERWHELMINGLY on May 5: $23.25M over three years, approved 8,675 to 5,732 with the highest-ever local turnout (34%+). The override funds schools ($17.94M) and town departments ($5.31M), avoiding hundreds of teacher layoffs and Fire Department cuts. Property taxes will increase approximately 18% over 3 years. The decisive margin (60% to 40%) shows strong community support for maintaining service levels.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ OVERRIDE PASSED OVERWHELMINGLY on May 5: $23.25M over three years, approved 8,675 to 5,732 with the highest-ever local turnout (34%+). The override funds schools ($17.94M) and town departments ($5.31M), avoiding hundreds of teacher layoffs and Fire Department cuts. Property taxes will increase approximately 18% over 3 years. The decisive margin (60% to 40%) shows strong community support for maintaining service levels.

**June 4 update:** MAJOR — Town Meeting approved Route 9 rezoning 217-20 (well above two-thirds threshold) for City Realty Group's mixed-use development: 3 buildings, 200-room hotel, 266 apartments. Largest development in Brookline's history. 26 Pleasant Street (103 apartments, 15 affordable) POSTPONED to fall Town Meeting. $23.5M operating override on ballot. Town Meeting continues through June 2-4 if needed.

**June 22 update:** No new developments. Override approved ($23.25M over three years). Implementation underway — funds schools ($17.94M) and town departments ($5.31M).

**June 23 update:** Override APPROVED $23.25M over 3 years by vote of 8,675-5,732. Highest-ever local turnout (34%). Override funds now secured for FY2027-FY2029.

**June 28 update:** Budget override approved — $23.25M over 3 years for schools and town services. Implementation underway for FY2027.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor Select Board meetings for override proposal details. If placed on ballot, vote.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Override votes determine whether Brookline can maintain service quality or faces austerity cuts. The fiscal math is straightforward: if the town can't raise revenue, it must cut spending — typically hitting schools and infrastructure first. For a pro-housing perspective, the fiscal pressure also creates an incentive for development: more housing = more property tax revenue = less override pressure. Support the override if it funds genuine priorities, and use the fiscal conversation to advocate for housing production as a structural budget solution.

issues.title: MBTA Communities Act Compliance — Brookline's Multifamily Zoning Obligation
issues.summary:
ONGOING (2026): Brookline, like all MBTA-adjacent communities in Massachusetts, must comply with the MBTA Communities Act (Section 3A of the Zoning Act), which requires communities served by the MBTA to zone for multifamily housing near transit stations. Brookline has Green Line stops (Brookline Village, Brookline Hills, Beaconsfield, Reservoir, Chestnut Hill) and must designate areas where multifamily housing is allowed by right. Compliance has been a major flashpoint — some Town Meeting members have pushed back against the state mandate, viewing it as an infringement on local control. However, non-compliance risks loss of state funding for housing, infrastructure, and other programs. The zoning articles in the May 2026 Town Meeting warrant may include provisions related to MBTA Communities Act compliance.

**May 19 update:** Town Meeting zoning articles on MAY 26 (6 DAYS AWAY) may include MBTA Communities provisions. With the Select Board also adding a Special Town Meeting to consider Chestnut Hill West rezoning, the compliance question is directly on the agenda. This is the key vote to watch.

**May 23 update:** Town Meeting is 3 DAYS AWAY (May 26). The Special Town Meeting's Chestnut Hill West rezoning vote (CHC Overlay District — 27.8 acres, buildings up to 150-175 feet) is directly related to MBTA Communities compliance. A two-thirds majority is needed. This is the key vote that determines whether Brookline advances toward compliance or remains in a confrontational posture with the state.

**May 24 update:** Town Meeting begins May 26 — 2 DAYS AWAY. The CHC Overlay District vote is the key MBTA Communities compliance test.

**May 25 update:** ⚡ Town Meeting starts TOMORROW (May 26). The CHC Overlay District vote (expected Thursday May 28) is the key MBTA Communities compliance test. 42 TM members from South Brookline have registered formal opposition — a significant bloc when two-thirds majority is required. The outcome determines whether Brookline advances toward compliance with 266 new housing units or remains in a confrontational posture with the state.

**May 26 update:** ⚡ Town Meeting starts TONIGHT. The CHC Overlay District vote (expected Thursday May 28, 2 DAYS) is the key MBTA Communities compliance test. 42 TM members from South Brookline have registered formal opposition — a significant bloc when two-thirds majority is required. A rival developer's last-minute objection adds another variable. Attend in person for close votes.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: CHC Overlay District vote TONIGHT at Town Meeting. This is the direct test of MBTA Communities compliance. Proponents appear to have the votes after a recent agreement with neighboring residents. Two-thirds majority required. If approved, 266 new housing units advance Brookline's compliance posture.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ CHC OVERLAY DISTRICT APPROVED 217-20 on May 29. This is a direct victory for MBTA Communities compliance — 266 new housing units in a transit-adjacent corridor on Route 9. The overwhelming margin (far exceeding the two-thirds threshold) signals that Brookline's political center has shifted decisively toward compliance. ADU updates (WA14) and additional zoning amendments (WA15) also expected to pass with strong support. Brookline is now advancing from grudging compliance to proactive development.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ CHC OVERLAY DISTRICT APPROVED 217-20 on May 29. This is a direct victory for MBTA Communities compliance — 266 new housing units in a transit-adjacent corridor on Route 9. The overwhelming margin (far exceeding the two-thirds threshold) signals that Brookline's political center has shifted decisively toward compliance. ADU updates (WA14) and additional zoning amendments (WA15) also expected to pass with strong support. Brookline is now advancing from grudging compliance to proactive development.

**June 4 update:** MAJOR — Town Meeting approved Route 9 rezoning 217-20 (well above two-thirds threshold) for City Realty Group's mixed-use development: 3 buildings, 200-room hotel, 266 apartments. Largest development in Brookline's history. 26 Pleasant Street (103 apartments, 15 affordable) POSTPONED to fall Town Meeting. $23.5M operating override on ballot. Town Meeting continues through June 2-4 if needed.

**June 10 update:** Brookline is NOT among the 9 towns the AG sued in January 2026 for MBTA Communities noncompliance — 165 of 177 communities have achieved compliance or conditional status. However, results so far are modest: only one known project in the MBTA Communities rezoning corridor (a childcare center plus 3 housing units). The AG approved Brookline's new zoning by-laws on March 23, 2026. A Housing Advisory Board subcommittee drafted a warrant article for Spring 2026 Town Meeting to address additional compliance measures.

**June 11 update:** Brookline remains compliant — AG approved new zoning by-laws March 23, 2026. Not among the 9 towns sued in January 2026. 165 of 177 communities have achieved compliance or conditional status. Results modest so far: only one known project in the rezoning corridor (childcare center + 3 housing units). Town Meeting approved Chestnut Hill West rezoning (May 28) and updated ADU rules (Article 14) — both add density capacity. The 26 Pleasant Street rezoning (103 units) postponed to fall, which would have been the most significant MBTA-corridor housing project to date.

**June 12 update:** Brookline remains compliant — AG approved new zoning by-laws March 23, 2026. Not among the 9 towns sued in January 2026 for noncompliance. 165 of 177 communities have achieved compliance or conditional status. Results remain modest: only one known project in the rezoning corridor (childcare center + 3 housing units). Town Meeting approved Chestnut Hill West rezoning and updated ADU rules — both add density capacity. The 26 Pleasant Street rezoning (103 units) postponed to fall Special Town Meeting.

**June 13 update:** Brookline remains compliant — AG approved new zoning by-laws March 23, 2026. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. Town Meeting approved Chestnut Hill West rezoning and updated ADU rules — adding density capacity. 26 Pleasant Street (103 units) postponed to fall Special Town Meeting. No further actions until fall session.

**June 14 update:** Brookline remains compliant — AG approved new zoning by-laws March 23. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. CHC Overlay District approved, ADU rules updated. 26 Pleasant Street (103 units) postponed to fall Special Town Meeting. No further actions until fall session.

**June 15 update:** Brookline remains compliant. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. No further actions until fall Special Town Meeting.

**June 16 update:** Brookline remains compliant. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. No further actions until fall Special Town Meeting.

**June 17 update:** Brookline remains compliant. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. No further actions until fall Special Town Meeting. Community Housing funding review TODAY at 2 PM on Zoom.

**June 18 update:** Brookline remains compliant. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. No further actions until fall Special Town Meeting. Community Housing funding review held yesterday (June 17).

**June 19 update:** Brookline remains compliant. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. No further actions until fall Special Town Meeting.

**June 22 update:** Brookline remains compliant. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance. No further actions until fall Special Town Meeting.

**June 23 update:** Brookline remains in full compliance with the MBTA Communities Act. The AG approved Brookline's zoning by-laws on March 23, 2026, and the town is not among the 9 communities sued for noncompliance. The CHC Overlay District approval (217-20) significantly advances Brookline's compliance posture by adding 266 housing units in a transit-adjacent corridor along Route 9. 165 of 177 communities statewide have achieved compliance or conditional status. Next compliance milestone: 26 Pleasant Street (103 units) at fall Special Town Meeting.

**June 28 update:** Brookline in full compliance with MBTA Communities Act. Implementation and permitting continuing under new zoning framework.

public_comment.instructions: Town Meeting zoning articles may address MBTA Communities compliance. Attend or participate remotely.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: MBTA Communities Act compliance is THE housing issue in Brookline. It forces the town to confront its historically exclusionary zoning near transit stations. As a YIMBY-curious resident, this is where your engagement has the most leverage — supporting compliance means supporting housing production near transit. The state's enforcement mechanism (withholding funding) creates real consequences for non-compliance, but political will at Town Meeting is what determines how generously or grudgingly Brookline zones for multifamily housing.

issues.title: Green Line Service and Extension — Transit Reliability Impacts Brookline
issues.summary:
ONGOING (2026): The MBTA Green Line, which serves Brookline via the C and D branches, continues to face reliability challenges. The Green Line Extension (GLX) to Somerville and Medford, which opened in stages in 2023-2024, has increased ridership on the system overall but also created capacity pressure on the existing branches. For Brookline, Green Line reliability directly affects commute quality, property values, and the viability of transit-oriented development near stations. The MBTA's ongoing capital improvement program includes signal upgrades, vehicle procurement, and station accessibility improvements that affect Brookline stops.

**June 22 update:** No new developments. Green Line service continues with ongoing capital improvement program.

**June 28 update:** Green Line service and extension impacts continue. Route 9 transportation working group being established with Newton as part of CHC overlay implementation.

public_comment.instructions: MBTA public meetings and comment periods at mbta.com. Advocacy through TransitMatters and other regional transit organizations.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Green Line reliability is the transit infrastructure backbone for Brookline. Better transit service makes MBTA Communities Act compliance more palatable politically — it's easier to support housing near transit when the transit actually works. As a tech professional who values efficiency, MBTA performance data is worth tracking as a barometer of regional governance quality.
